label: no
cot_prefix: Okay, let's read this carefully. The story is about Mr. Williams, a careful driver. He comes to a crossroad with a "SLOW" sign. He doesn't completely stop, just slows down, looks both ways and drives across. Then he hears a police whistle and stops. The policeman says he didn't stop at the crossing. Mr. Williams argues that the sign said "SLOW," not "STOP," and he did slow down. The policeman realizes he's in the wrong street. The question is asking what Mr. Williams was doing when he was stopped by the policeman. The options are: (A) was driving at a high speed (B) was driving onto the main road (C) was going to stop his car (D) was driving slowly Let me break it down. The key is what he was doing when stopped. According to the story, when he came to the main road (with the "SLOW" sign), he slowed down, looked both ways, and then drove across without stopping completely. So he was driving slowly when he crossed, not at high speed (so A is out). He had already passed the crossroad, and was driving across when the policeman whistled. But the problem is about what was he doing when he was stopped. The policeman stopped him because he didn't stop at the crossing, but according to Mr. Williams, he only had to slow down. So the issue is he was driving across the main road when he was stopped. Wait, the timing is a bit tricky. When was he stopped? The story says "At once he heard a police whistle, so he pulled into the side of the road and stopped." So he was on the main road, driving across, and the policeman whistled, so he pulled over. Therefore, when he was stopped by the policeman, he was driving onto the main road? Or had already been on it? The key sentence is: "He slowed down when he came to the main road, looked both ways to see that nothing was coming,
